passages.title: A New Ice Age
passages.source_html:
A New Ice Age


A 


William Curry is a serious, sober climate scientist, not an art critic. But he has spent a lot of time perusing Emanuel Gottlieb Leutze’s famous painting ‘George Washington Crossing the Delaware,’ which depicts a boatload of colonial American soldiers making their way to attack English and Hessian troops the day after Christmas in 1776. ‘Most people think these other guys in the boat are rowing, but they are actually pushing the ice away,’ says Curry, tapping his finger on a reproduction of the painting. Sure enough, the lead oarsman is bashing the frozen river with his boot. ‘I grew up in Philadelphia. The place in this painting is 30 minutes away by car. I can tell you, this kind of thing just doesn’t happen anymore.’


B 


But it may again soon. And ice-choked scenes, similar to those immortalized by the 16th-century Flemish painter Pieter Brueghel the Elder, may also return to Europe. His works, including the 1565 masterpiece ‘Hunters in the Snow,’ make the now-temperate European landscapes look more like Lapland. Such frigid settings were commonplace during a period dating roughly from 1300 to 1850 because much of North America and Europe was in the throes of a little ice age. And now there is mounting evidence that the chill could return. A growing number of scientists believe conditions are ripe for another prolonged cooldown, or small ice age. While no one is predicting a brutal ice sheet like the one that covered the Northern Hemisphere with glaciers about 12,000 years ago, the next cooling trend could drop average temperatures 5 degrees Fahrenheit over much of the United States and 10 degrees in the Northeast, northern Europe, and northern Asia.


C 


‘It could happen in 10 years,’ says Terrence Joyce, who chairs the Woods Hole Physical Oceanography Department. ‘Once it does, it can take hundreds of years to reverse.’ And he is alarmed that Americans have yet to take the threat seriously.


D 


A drop of 5 to 10 degrees entails much more than simply bumping up the thermostat and carrying on. Both economically and ecologically, such quick, persistent chilling could have devastating consequences. A 2002 report titled ‘Abrupt Climate Change: Inevitable Surprises,’ produced by the National Academy of Sciences, pegged the cost from agricultural losses alone at $100 billion to $250 billion while also predicting that damage to ecologies could be vast and incalculable. A grim sampler: disappearing forests, increased housing expenses, dwindling freshwater, lower crop yields, and accelerated species extinctions.


E 


Political changes since the last ice age could make survival far more difficult for the world’s poor. During previous cooling periods, whole tribes simply picked up and moved south, but that option doesn’t work in the modern, tense world of closed borders. ‘To the extent that abrupt climate change may cause rapid and extensive changes of fortune for those who live off the land, the inability to migrate may remove one of the major safety nets for distressed people,’ says the report.


F 


But first things first. Isn’t the earth actually warming? In deed it is, says Joyce. In his cluttered office, full of soft light from the foggy Cape Cod morning, he explains how such warming could actually be the surprising culprit of the next mini-ice age. The paradox is a result of the appearance over the past 30 years in the North Atlantic of huge rivers of freshwater – the equivalent of a 10-foot-thick layer – mixed into the salty sea. No one is certain where the fresh torrents are coming from, but a prime suspect is melting Arctic ice, caused by a buildup of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere that traps solar energy.


G 


The freshwater trend is major news in ocean-science circles. Bob Dickson, a British oceanographer who sounded an alarm at a February conference in Honolulu, has termed the drop in salinity and temperature in the Labrador Sea – a body of water between northeastern Canada and Greenland that adjoins the Atlantic – ‘arguably the largest full-depth changes observed in the modem instrumental oceanographic record.’ The trend could cause a little ice age by subverting the northern penetration of Gulf Stream waters.


H 


Normally, the Gulf Stream, laden with heat soaked up in the tropics, meanders up the east coasts of the United States and Canada. As it flows northward, the stream surrenders heat to the air. Because the prevailing North Atlantic winds blow eastward, a lot of the heat wafts to Europe. That’s why many scientists believe winter temperatures on the Continent are as much as 36 degrees Fahrenheit warmer than those in North America at the same latitude. Frigid Boston, for example, lies at almost precisely the same latitude as balmy Rome. And some scientists say the heat also warms Americans and Canadians. ‘It’s a real mistake to think of this solely as a European phenomenon,’ says Joyce.


I 


Having given up its heat to the air, the now-cooler water becomes denser and sinks into the North Atlantic by a mile or more in a process oceanographers call thermohaline circulation. This massive column of cascading cold is the main engine powering a deepwater current called the Great Ocean Conveyor that snakes through all the world’s oceans. But as the North Atlantic fills with freshwater, it grows less dense, making the waters carried northward by the Gulf Stream less able to sink. The new mass of relatively freshwater sits on top of the ocean like a big thermal blanket, threatening the thermohaline circulation. That in turn could make the Gulf Stream slow or veer southward. At some point, the whole system could simply shut down, and do so quickly. ‘There is increasing evidence that we are getting closer toa transition point, from which we can jump to a new state. Small changes, such as a couple of years of heavy precipitation or melting ice at high latitudes, could yield a big response,’ says Joyce.


J 


‘You have all this freshwater sitting at high latitudes, and it can literally take hundreds of years to get rid of it,’ Joyce says. So while the globe as a whole gets warmer by tiny fractions of 1 degree Fahrenheit annually, the North Atlantic region could, in a decade, get up to 10 degrees colder. What worries researchers at Woods Hole is that history is on the side of rapid shutdown. They know it has happened before.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14-16 


Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D. Write your answers in boxes 14-16 on your answer sheet.

 


14

The writer mentions the paintings in the first two paragraphs to illustrate

正确答案： D. the possibility of a small ice age in the future.

原文答案句：But it may again soon. And ice-choked scenes, similar to those immortalized by the 16th-century Flemish painter Pieter Brueghel the Elder, may also return to Europe.  

对应段落：B段  

审题：题目问作者在文章前两段提到画作是为了说明什么。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"the paintings in the first two paragraphs"定位到A段和 B段中关于画作的内容。A段提到华盛顿渡河的画作，B段提到Pieter Brueghel the Elder的画作，并提到类似的冰封场景可能再次出现。  

解题思路：A段提到华盛顿渡河的画作，说明过去河面结冰的情景；B段提到Pieter Brueghel the Elder的画作，并指出类似的冰封场景可能再次出现（"may also return to Europe"），暗示未来可能再次出现小冰河时期。因此，作者提到这些画作是为了说明未来可能再次出现小冰河时期，对应选项D。选项A和B与画作本身无关，选项C提到的12,000年前的小冰河时期在B段中只是作为对比，不是画作要说明的重点。


15

Why is it hard for the poor to survive the next cooling period?

正确答案： C. because migration seems impossible for the reason of closed borders.

原文答案句：During previous cooling periods, whole tribes simply picked up and moved south, but that option doesn’t work in the modern, tense world of closed borders.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问的是为什么穷人难以在下一个冷却期生存。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词“the poor”和“survive the next cooling period”可以定位到E段，该段讨论了在冷却期穷人难以生存的原因。

解题思路：根据原文答案句可以看出，在过去的冷却期，部落可以简单地迁移到南方，但在现代紧张的世界中，由于边境封闭，这种选择不再可行。因此，穷人难以在下一个冷却期生存的原因是迁移似乎因为边境封闭而变得不可能。这与选项C的描述一致。


16

Why is the winter temperature in continental Europe higher than that in North America?

正确答案： A. because heat is brought to Europe with the wind flow.

原文答案句：Normally, the Gulf Stream, laden with heat soaked up in the tropics, meanders up the east coasts of the United States and Canada. As it flows northward, the stream surrenders heat to the air. Because the prevailing North Atlantic winds blow eastward, a lot of the heat wafts to Europe. 

对应段落：H段  

审题：题目问为什么欧洲大陆的冬季气温比北美洲高。  

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词"higher winter temperature in continental Europe than in North America"定位到H段，该段解释了欧洲气温较高的原因。  

解题思路：原文明确指出，墨西哥湾暖流携带着热带吸收的热量，蜿蜒流经美国和加拿大的东海岸。当它向北流动时，暖流向空气中释放热量。由于盛行的北大西洋风向东风，大量热量飘向欧洲。因此，正确答案是A，因为热量通过风向流动被带到欧洲。


Questions 17-21 


Match each statement (Questions 17-21) with the correct person A-D in the box below. Write the correct letter, A, B, C or D, in boxes 17-21 on your answer sheet. 


NB You may use any letter more than once.


List of People 

A Bob Dickson 

B Terrence Joyce 

C William Curry 

D National Academy of Science 


Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
17

17 A quick climate change wreaks great disruption.
✅ 正确答案：D

原文答案句：A 2002 report titled ‘Abrupt Climate Change: Inevitable Surprises,’ produced by the National Academy of Sciences, pegged the cost from agricultural losses alone at $100 billion to $250 billion while also predicting that damage to ecologies could be vast and incalculable.  

对应段落：D段  

审题：题目问的是“剧烈的气候变化会造成巨大破坏”这一观点是谁提出的。  

定位关键信息：题目中的关键词是"quick climate change"和"great disruption"，在文章中找到相关表述。  

解题思路：D段提到国家科学院的报告《气候突变：不可避免的意外》报告指出，仅农业损失一项就高达1000亿至2500亿美元，同时预测生态破坏可能极其严重且难以估量。这表明剧烈的气候变化会导致巨大破坏，因此，答案为D。
18

18 Most Americans are not prepared for the next cooling period.
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：‘It could happen in 10 years,’ says Terrence Joyce, who chairs the Woods Hole Physical Oceanography Department. ‘Once it does, it can take hundreds of years to reverse.’ And he is alarmed that Americans have yet to take the threat seriously.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是“大多数美国人没有为下一次降温做好准备”，这一观点是谁提出的。

定位关键信息：根据题目中的关键词“Americans are not prepared for the next cooling period”，可以在C段中找到“Americans have yet to take the threat seriously”，意为“美国人至今仍未将这一威胁视为严重问题”。

解题思路：原文中的句子表明，伍兹霍尔物理海洋学系主任特伦斯·乔伊斯表示：‘这种现象可能在十年内发生，一旦出现，可能需要数百年才能逆转。’他担忧的是，美国人至今仍未将这一威胁视为严重问题，与题目意思一致。因此，答案为B。
19

19 A case of a change of ocean water is mentioned in a conference.
✅ 正确答案：A

原文答案句：The freshwater trend is major news in ocean-science circles. Bob Dickson, a British oceanographer who sounded an alarm at a February conference in Honolulu, has termed the drop in salinity and temperature in the Labrador Sea – a body of water between northeastern Canada and Greenland that adjoins the Atlantic – ‘arguably the largest full-depth changes observed in the modem instrumental oceanographic record.’  

对应段落：G段  

审题：题目问，是谁“在会议中提及了一起海水变化的案例”。  

定位关键信息：根据题目中的关键词“change of ocean water”和“a conference”可以定位到G段，该段提到了Bob Dickson在檀香山的一个会议上提出了关于海洋水盐度和温度变化的问题。  

解题思路：G段中明确提到了Bob Dickson在会议上讨论了Labrador海的水体盐度和温度下降的问题，并称其为“现代海洋观测记录中观察到的最大深度变化之一”。这直接对应了题目中“海洋水变化”和“会议”的描述，因此答案为A。
20

20 Global warming urges the appearance of the ice age.
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：In deed it is, says Joyce. In his cluttered office, full of soft light from the foggy Cape Cod morning, he explains how such warming could actually be the surprising culprit of the next mini-ice age. 

对应段落：F段  

审题：题目问“全球变暖促使冰河时代的出现”，这一观点是谁提出的。  

定位关键信息：通过“global warming”和“ice age”定位到F段，该段提到全球变暖（melting Arctic ice, caused by a buildup of carbon dioxide）与冰河时代的关系。  

解题思路：F段中，Joyce解释说这种变暖现象可能正是引发下一个迷你冰河时代的惊人元凶。全球变暖（北极冰融化）导致淡水进入北大西洋，而淡水层可能引发冰河时代。因此，Joyce的观点直接关联了全球变暖与冰河时代的可能性，对应选项B。
21

21 The temperature will not drop to the same degree as it used to be.
✅ 正确答案：C

原文答案句：‘Most people think these other guys in the boat are rowing, but they are actually pushing the ice away,’ says Curry, tapping his finger on a reproduction of the painting. Sure enough, the lead oarsman is bashing the frozen river with his boot. ‘I grew up in Philadelphia. The place in this painting is 30 minutes away by car. I can tell you, this kind of thing just doesn’t happen anymore.’

对应段落：A段  

审题：题目问的是，气温不会像以往那样大幅下降，这一观点是谁提出的。  

定位关键信息：通过题目的关键词“temperature will not drop to the same degree as it used to be”定位到A段。

解题思路：A段主要讲了Curry对伊曼纽尔·戈特利布·勒特兹的名画《乔治·华盛顿渡过特拉华河》的研究，并且他明确说明，多数人以为画中人正在划桨，其实他们是在用船桨推开冰面，这种场景如今已不复存在了。说明他认为以后不会再有这样的大降温，与题目表述一致，因此，选择C项。

Questions 22-26 


Complete the flow chart below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

 

22

22

正确答案： 22 heat
Question 22

原文答案句：As it flows northward, the stream surrenders heat to the air.  

对应段落：H段  

审题：题目要求根据流程图完成填空，需要填写的是热量传递过程中的一个关键环节。  

定位关键信息：通过流程图的结构和上下文，可以确定题目在询问热量传递的过程。

解题思路：在H段中，原文明确提到墨西哥湾暖流，向北流动时，会将热量释放到空气中（"surrenders heat to the air"）。也就是将热量蒸发到空气中去，因此，热量（heat）是这一环节的关键词，符合流程图中热量传递的描述。


23

23 

正确答案： 23 denser
Question 23

原文答案句：Having given up its heat to the air, the now-cooler water becomes denser and sinks into the North Atlantic by a mile or more in a process oceanographers call thermohaline circulation.

对应段落：I段

审题：题目要求完成流程图，根据上下文推断此处询问的是水在失去热量后的变化状态。

定位关键信息：利用流程图上下文提示，定位到I段描述水在释放热量后的状态变化。

解题思路：根据原文I段中“the now-cooler water becomes denser”水体释放热量后，温度降低的海水密度增大，可以直接确定答案为"denser"，海水密度增大。


24

24 

正确答案： 24 Great Ocean Conveyor
Question 24

原文答案句：This massive column of cascading cold is the main engine powering a deepwater current called the Great Ocean Conveyor that snakes through all the world’s oceans.

对应段落：I段

审题：题目要求完成一个流程图，需要填入一个不超过三个词的名词词组，表示一个海洋环流系统的名称。

定位关键信息：根据流程图上下文，题目需要填入的是海洋环流系统的名称。在文章中寻找描述海洋环流系统的句子，重点关注I段中关于海洋环流系统的描述。

解题思路：I段中提到“this massive column of cascading cold is the main engine powering a deepwater current called the Great Ocean Conveyor”，这股巨大的冷空气团是驱动深海水流的主要动力，这种被称为“大海洋输送带”的水流蜿蜒穿过全球所有海洋。这句话明确指出了这个海洋环流系统的名称是“Great Ocean Conveyor”。因此，根据原文信息，答案为“Great Ocean Conveyor”。


25

25 

正确答案： 25 freshwater
Question 25

原文答案句：But as the North Atlantic fills with freshwater, it grows less dense, making the waters carried northward by the Gulf Stream less able to sink. The new mass of relatively freshwater sits on top of the ocean like a big thermal blanket, threatening the thermohaline circulation. That in turn could make the Gulf Stream slow or veer southward.

对应段落：I段

审题：题目要求根据文章内容完成流程图，其中需要填写一个不超过三个词的答案，因为什么的增加，导致影响力墨西哥湾暖流。

定位关键信息：根据流程图的上下文，可以定位到文章中关于墨西哥湾暖流的影响。

解题思路：I段提到，随着北大西洋淡水的不断注入，海水密度逐渐降低，墨西哥湾暖流向北输送的海水也难以下沉。这些相对淡水的新堆积层如同厚重的保温毯覆盖在海面，威胁着盐温环流的正常运作。这里提到了淡水的增加，后文紧接着提到对盐温环流的影响，间接影响了墨西哥湾暖流。因此，填入淡水，freshwater。


26

26 

正确答案： 26 southward
Question 26

原文答案句：The new mass of relatively freshwater sits on top of the ocean like a big thermal blanket, threatening the thermohaline circulation. That in turn could make the Gulf Stream slow or veer southward. At some point, the whole system could simply shut down, and do so quickly. 

对应段落：I段

审题：题目要求根据流程图完成填空，需要填写墨西哥湾暖流可能发生的变化方向。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"Gulf Stream"定位到I段，找到"Gulf Stream slow or veer southward"这一关键信息。

解题思路：根据原文I段中的句子"make the Gulf Stream slow or veer southward"可以确定，当淡水增多时，墨西哥湾暖流可能会减速，甚至向南移动，因此答案为"southward"。
